narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you bring from your background. Your profile includes a middle family income bracket, full-time student status, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 30, and school deciles of 4th in both your first and third years—placing you below the typical student's averages across these measures. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on similar student data, estimated your probability of failing the bar at 41.4%, a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it weighed several key factors from your record, with family income having the strongest positive sway toward expecting you to pass, likely because middle-income backgrounds in the dataset often correlate with solid preparation and support. Your third-year school decile ranking also nudged the prediction toward passing, suggesting your later academic performance showed some resilience compared to peers. Being a full-time student further helped, as most successful bar takers in the data share this commitment, aligning you with the vast majority at 92.7%. On the flip side, your first-year decile had a notable pull toward the failure prediction, indicating an early struggle that stood out more than other elements. Your LSAT and undergraduate GPA offered minor boosts toward passing but weren't enough to shift the balance significantly. Overall, while some aspects like your family background positioned you favorably against typical applicants, the early academic decile tipped the scales just enough toward concern. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the average of 36.7, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework, and highlighting any upward trends or personal achievements in a reapplication. We're rooting for you to build on your strengths and reapply stronger.
